one_line_takeaway: RTBAS adapts information-flow control to LLM tool-agents by using LM-judge or attention-based dependency screeners to redact only the history regions that matter, blocking 100% of policy-violating AgentDojo injections at <2% utility loss and cutting privacy-leak false alarms from ~30% (confirm-every-call) to ~8% while matching an oracle.
key_finding: Under prompt injection on AgentDojo, RTBAS prevents 100% of policy-violating attacks while losing under ~1% task utility for the LM-Judge screener and ~3% for the attention screener versus the no-defense baseline (paper headlines <2% degradation), outperforming the AgentDojo Tool Filter SOTA. On the privacy-leakage benchmark, selective propagation sits between the two extremes: LM-Judge achieves 8.1% FPR and 10.8% FNR (attention: 16.2% FPR, 10.8% FNR), versus Confirm-Never (0% FPR but 51.4% FNR) and Confirm-Every-Time/GPTs (29.7% FPR, 0% FNR), matching the oracle's tool-call decisions on all but 1 of 15 flight-booking cases (an over-tainting error). The paper also shows attention scores from a small open model reliably separate dependent from non-dependent regions even for closed-source GPT-4o/Claude agents.
core_contribution: RTBAS (Robust Tool-Based Agent System) is an information-flow-control (IFC) framework that defends LLM tool-using agents against prompt injection (integrity violations) and privacy leakage (confidentiality violations) while avoiding the user-fatigue of confirm-every-tool-call defenses (e.g., OpenAI GPTs). The paper's own novel contributions are: (1) adapting dynamic taint-tracking/IFC to the dynamic, opaque TBAS setting by attaching integrity+confidentiality labels to message 'regions' and propagating them through a security lattice; (2) two 'dependency screeners' that decide which regions of the LM history actually influence the next tool call/response so that only those labels are joined and irrelevant regions are redacted/masked, combating IFC 'label creep'. The two screeners are an LM-as-a-judge screener and an attention-based screener (a lightweight 2-layer LSTM trained on attention features from a small open-source model). NOTE: 'Information Flow for LLMs [40]' and 'attention scores as saliency [43,49]' are cited prior work the paper builds on/contrasts with, not RTBAS's own contribution — the paper explicitly argues [40] needs exponential subset enumeration and does not handle interactive tool-calling, whereas RTBAS's screener runs in a single call.
threat_model: Attacker plants malicious content in data sources that untrusted tools later read (e.g., a Venmo transaction description, a fetched website, public reviews/comments) — i.e., indirect prompt injection originating from tool responses, not user-initiated jailbreaks. The attacker knows the TBAS system prompt, available tools, and per-tool instructions, but cannot compromise the tool implementation itself, cannot intercept/alter tool<->agent communication, cannot see user inputs or tool arguments/responses, and has no knowledge of timing or the agent's internal state. Attacker goals rely on side effects of integrity-sensitive tool calls: unauthorized money transfers, unwanted reservations/bookings, or exfiltrating confidential data (PII, credit card, passport) through public channels. Confidentiality threat also includes accidental (non-malicious) leakage where underspecified user requests or tool descriptions cause the agent to disclose private context data.
limitations: Security is only as good as the developer-provided labels and policy — the paper reports one AgentDojo workspace case where user-authored high-integrity text contained an injection that was therefore not tracked, so guarantees can be bypassed by mislabeling. Labeling messages/tools and authoring a user-understandable IFC policy is acknowledged as an open IFC burden on developers. Slack suite is a weak spot: utility drops to 33% (attention) / 22% (LM-Judge) because tasks inherently mix untrusted web content into high-integrity actions, which the authors deem inherently unsafe and requiring confirmation. Runtime overhead is substantial — RTBAS roughly doubles latency and token cost (attention screener needs two LM passes). The attention classifier is trained on only 40 AgentDojo cases (85% train / 81% test accuracy) raising generalization concern; small local models struggled as general-purpose screeners. The synthesized privacy benchmark is small (37 hand-made cases) and its confirm/no-confirm labels are manually assigned. Evaluations do not model actual user confirmation behavior (suspicious calls are simply skipped).

datasets_used: AgentDojo prompt-injection benchmark: 79 user tasks across 4 suites (Banking 16, Travel 20, Slack 21, Workspace 40); per-suite test cases Banking 144 / Travel 140 / Slack 105 / Workspace 240; 629 total security test cases under attack
Synthesized 'Accidental Leakage' privacy benchmark: 37 manually-created test cases across 3 domains (Venmo 12 tasks, Flight Booking 12 tasks, Amazon 13 tasks) with oracle confirm/no-confirm labels
Attention-screener training set: 40 well-labeled AgentDojo test cases
Motivating attention case studies: 3,424 input-argument/tool-call dependency pairs and 2,416 labeled instruction-following data points
quantitative_results: Prompt injection: 100% of policy-violating attacks prevented (one workspace case escaped only because user-authored text was labeled high-integrity)
Under-attack utility loss vs no-defense baseline: <1% (LM-Judge), ~3% (attention); headline <2% degradation
No-attack utility degradation vs no-defense baseline: 10% (LM-Judge), 7.4% (attention)
Prompt-injection weighted-average utility: no-defense 0.40, naive-tainting 0.50, redact-everything 0.51, tool-filter 0.53, RTBAS Attn/LM-Judge 0.58
Prompt-injection weighted-average integrity: no-defense 0.56, tool-filter 0.93, RTBAS ~0.99
Privacy FPR/FNR (Accidental Leakage): Confirm-Never 0 / 0.514; Confirm-Every-Time (GPTs) 0.297 / 0; RTBAS LM-Judge 0.081 / 0.108; RTBAS Attention 0.162 / 0.108
Per-suite FNR reduction (LM-Judge vs Confirm-Never): Amazon 30.7%->7.6%, Flight Booking 58.3%->8.3%, Venmo 66.7%->16.6%
Exact private-label match rate: Redact-All 22.3%, GPTs 56.7%, RTBAS Attention 57.3%, RTBAS LM-Judge 70.0%
Privacy utility (block-on-confirm baseline): LM-Judge 40%, attention 43%; near-oracle, losing only 1 of 15 cases; allow-all utility 83% (LM-Judge) / 75% (attention) vs GPT 83%
Attention classifier: 85% train / 81% test accuracy; 2-layer LSTM
Runtime on banking suite (avg/case): Vanilla $0.0147, 4.37s, 2710 tokens; Tool Filter $0.0087, 4.88s, 1505 tokens; RTBAS Attn $0.0275, 8.73s, 5049 tokens; RTBAS LM-Judge $0.0317, 9.71s, 5852 tokens
Attention-mass below 0.2 for non-dependent data 74-86% vs dependent data only 14% (GPT-4o) / 44% (Claude)
baselines_compared: Tool Filter (AgentDojo LM-as-judge tool restriction; treated as prior SOTA)
Naive Tainting (assume every history region taints the next message)
Redact Everything / Confirm-Never Redact-All (redact all non-high-integrity/non-public regions)
Confirm Every Time (OpenAI GPTs, confirm every tool call)
Oracle (human expert deciding tool calls and confirmations)
Referenced-but-excluded (evaluated by AgentDojo, worse than Tool Filter): PI Detector [3], Delimiting [13], Prompt Sandwiching [38] (27% attack success rate)
